title: Arbitrarily Slow Decay in the Mobius Disjointness Conjecture
speaker: Zhiren Wang
affiliation: von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: We construct examples showing that the correlation in the Mobius disjointness conjecture can go to zero arbitrarily slowly. In fact, our methods yield a more general result, where in lieu of μ(n) one can put any bounded sequence such that the Cesàro mean of the corresponding sequence of absolute values does not tend to zero. This is a joint work with Amir Algom.
tags: Workshop on Dynamics, Discrete Analysis and Multiplicative Number Theory

proof_stages.label: Sarnak disjointness and the rate problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Sarnak's Möbius-disjointness conjecture for zero-entropy systems, known positive criteria from slow complexity, and reasons to expect that zero entropy should not imply a universal quantitative decay rate.

proof_stages.label: Uniform arbitrarily slow-decay theorem
proof_stages.summary: For any target rate tau, the claimed result constructs a zero-entropy system, map, and fixed continuous observable satisfying Sarnak disjointness, while correlations against any bounded nontrivial sequence can be forced to decay no faster than tau along suitable scales.

proof_stages.label: Slowdown of the full-shift correlator
proof_stages.summary: The construction starts from the full shift, which can realize arbitrary prescribed sequences, and replaces it by a skew-product shift whose fiber coordinate moves only sublinearly.

proof_stages.label: Phase-adjustable low-complexity base
proof_stages.summary: A rapidly increasing sequence of scales q_k is chosen, and base sequences with long shifted blocks of zeros and ones are arranged so that the cumulative fiber displacement forms small tents, yielding zero entropy and Sarnak-valid low complexity.

proof_stages.label: Reading the target sequence through the slow fiber
proof_stages.summary: For a target sequence a_n, first treated as plus-or-minus one, the fiber word is made to mimic a_n at positions selected by a base phase c and a fiber offset b, giving an explicit block-correlation formula.

proof_stages.label: Averaging and pigeonhole lower bound
proof_stages.summary: Averaging the block correlations over all offsets for moduli q and q−1 converts them to the self-correlation of a_n, so some choice of parameters gives a lower bound comparable to 1/q, which is chosen to dominate tau at the relevant scale.

proof_stages.label: Patching for the exact theorem and logarithmic version
proof_stages.summary: The final technical fixes combine the two moduli, cover the whole line for general bounded sequences using finitely many product copies and a switch, and modify the tent construction for logarithmic averages.

examples.name: Zero-entropy skew products on the two-torus
examples.note: This known family illustrates existing smoothness-dependent Sarnak and prime-orbit results and motivates asking whether any quantitative decay rate should be expected.
examples.engagement_reason: the two-torus skew products are introduced as a known zero-entropy family with cited Sarnak/prime results, but no concrete computation or construction is carried out on them

passes.reason: Zero entropy is the minimum entropy boundary, and low-regularity toral skew products are cited as possible failures of the prime-orbit analogue.
Zero entropy puts these toral skew products on the minimum-entropy boundary, and the nonsmooth/slow-decay variants are cited to defeat prime or uniform-rate expectations.
zero topological entropy is the minimum entropy case, and rough torus skew products are cited as possible failures of the prime-orbit analogue
zero entropy is the boundary/minimal-entropy case for toral skew products, and the low-regularity version is cited as a failure of stronger prime-orbit/rate expectations
Zero-entropy toral skew products lie on the minimum-entropy boundary and are invoked as rough examples where prime-orbit analogues/rate expectations can fail.

Cited as zero-entropy toral skew products where low smoothness can make prime-orbit/noncorrelation statements fail; zero entropy is the minimal entropy boundary.
Low-regularity zero-entropy torus skew products are cited as obstructions to related noncorrelation statements, and zero entropy is the minimal entropy boundary.
low-regularity zero-entropy torus skew products are cited as cases where a prime-orbit analogue can fail, and zero entropy is the minimal entropy boundary.
used as zero-entropy skew-product systems obstructing stronger uniform decay or prime-type conclusions, with zero entropy at the minimal entropy boundary
Rough zero-entropy torus skew products are cited as possible failures of the prime/disjointness analogue, while zero entropy is the minimal entropy boundary.

examples.name: Sparse sampling along n^2
examples.note: The polynomial subsequence n^2 is cited as a warning that zero entropy alone need not control correlations on sparse subsequences, reinforcing the no-universal-rate motivation.
examples.engagement_reason: sampling along n^2 is cited as evidence that zero entropy need not give disjointness along sparse polynomial sequences, without working through the example

passes.reason: The square subsequence is an ordinary polynomial sampling sequence, used to show zero entropy need not give disjointness along sparse times.
The square subsequence is a zero-density thinning of the full orbit and is used to show zero entropy need not give disjointness on sparse polynomial samples.
sampling on the square subsequence is a zero-density boundary regime used to show zero entropy need not give sparse-subsequence disjointness
the square subsequence is an ordinary polynomial sparse sampling scheme used to show zero entropy need not ensure noncorrelation on sparse subsequences
Sampling along n^2 is an ordinary polynomial sparse subsequence, used to show zero entropy does not guarantee disjointness along such sparse times.
the ordinary square subsequence is used to refute zero-entropy disjointness along sparse sampling
Used explicitly to show zero entropy does not ensure noncorrelation along the square times; the squares form a zero-density sparse sampling subsequence.
Sampling along the squares is used to show zero entropy need not give noncorrelation on sparse subsequences, and the square times form a zero-density boundary case.
sampling along the squares is used to show zero entropy need not imply noncorrelation on sparse subsequences, with zero-density sampling a boundary regime.
cited to show zero entropy does not guarantee Möbius noncorrelation after sparse sampling along the squares
Sampling along the ordinary quadratic subsequence n^2 is used to show zero entropy need not give noncorrelation on sparse subsequences.
sampling along the squares is cited to show zero entropy need not imply noncorrelation on sparse polynomial subsequences

examples.name: Irrational circle rotation as a possible simplification
examples.note: An audience member asks whether an irrational rotation with a very rough continuous observable could already realize the phenomenon; the speaker uses it only to clarify why the planned construction is more like a skew-product slowdown.
examples.engagement_reason: irrational circle rotations are discussed speculatively in response to a question as a possible simplification, but no property is checked or construction run on a rotation

passes.reason: An irrational circle rotation is a zero-entropy equicontinuous group rotation, raised only as a possible simplification.
An irrational circle rotation is a homogeneous isometric zero-entropy system, but here it is only proposed as a possible simpler model.
